coredata.dc:title: A hyaluronic acid Schiff base @ hollow calcium peroxide @ copper carbon dots nanoconjugate enhances chemodynamic tumor therapy based on multiple approaches strategy

coredata.dc:description: Chemodynamic therapy (CDT) is a highly promising cancer treatment strategy. However, its clinical application is severely limited due to insufficient H2O2 levels. To overcome this critical issues, based on self-supply H2O2 strategy this study designed a multifunctional nano-reactor hyaluronic acid (HA)-cinnamaldehyde(CA)Schiff base(HA-CA)@hollow CaO2(HMCaO2)/Copper doped luteolin carbon dots (CuLCDs) (abbreviation:HACOCLC). HACOCLC based on self-supplied H2O2/O2 strategy to enhance the anti-tumor effect. HACOCLC preferentially enrich at the tumor site under the tumor targeting effect of HA. pH stimulation triggered the release of HMCaO2 and CuLCDs from HACOCLC. HMCaO2 hydrolyzed to produce H2O2, Ca2+ and O2, H2O2 compensated substrates for CDT, O2 down-regulates hypoxia-inducing factor (HIF-1α) and programmed death ligand 1(PD-L1) to consolidate the therapeutic effect, and Ca2+ entered mitochondria to cause calcium overload and induced cell death. CuLCDs underwent Fenton-like reaction to produce •OH (CDT effect) and O2, which further increased the O2 level. CuLCDs has near-infrared light (808 nm) thermal conversion (conversion efficiency was 40.3 %) for photothermal therapy (PTT), Cu2+ depletion of glutathione amplified the level of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and improved the therapeutic effect. HACOCLC self-supplied H2O2 and O2 to enhance CDT, and effectively inhibited tumor growth through the combined action of CDT/PTT/ calcium overload.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of HACOCLC a) TEM and HRTEM of HMCaO2 b) XRD of HMCaO2 c) N2 adsorption-desorption curve of HMCaO2 d) TEM and HRTEM of HMCaO2 e) N2 adsorption-desorption curve of HMCaO2@CuLCDs f) Raman spectrum of HMCaO2 and HMCaO2@CuLCDs g) TEM of HACOCLC h) FT-IR spectrum of HMCaO2, HMCaO2@CuLCDs and HACOCLC i) Zeta potential of HMCaO2, HMCaO2@CuLCDs, HA-CA and HACOCLC j) XPS of HACOCLC k), l) XPS of Ca 2p and Cu 2p, respectively.

Morphological changes and photothermal conversion properties of HACOCLC a) HACOCLC morphology in different pH solutions b) Cumulative release profile of CuLCDs c) Absorption spectrum of HMCaO2, CuLCDs, HMCaO2@CuLCDs and HACOCLC d) Fluorescence emission spectrum of HACOCLC, λex = 350 nm e) Photothermal effect of HACOCLC under 808 nm laser (1.5 W cm−2) irradiation f) Photothermal conversion effect of HACOCLC under different light sources g) Heating-cooling curve controlled by the light source switch h) Light source switch 5 times heating and cooling cycle curve i) Heat transfer constant - light time linear correlation curve j) Photothermal imaging of HACOCLC. Data were presented as means ± standard deviation (SD) (n = 3), **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Evaluation of HACOCLC's ability to deplete GSH, evaluation of H2O2 production capacity of HA@HMCaO2@LCDs and HACOCLC a) DTNB was used to detect the GSH depletion capacity of HACOCLC at different concentrations. The absorbance of DTNB was proportional to the GSH depletion capacity of HACOCLC. b) The GSH consuming capacity of HACOCLC (40 μg mL−1) at different pH was measured by DTNB. c) Color change of TMB incubated with HRP, HA@HMCaO2@LCDs d) The discoloration mechanism of samples was detected by HRP-TMB e)Absorbance of TMB at various concentrations of HA@HMCaO2@LCDs f) Amount of hydrogen peroxide produced by different concentration HA@HMCaO2@LCDs g) H2O2 yield by HA@HMCaO2@LCDs hydrolysis h) Amount of H2O2 produced by HACOCLC i) Absorbance of MB in HACOCLC at various concentrations j) MB absorbance in HACOCLC at different pH. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Investigation of self-supplied O2 capacity of HACOCLC a) The oxygen production capacity of different concentrations of HMCaO2(0, 0.71, 10.7, 14.1, 17.8 and 21.4 μg mL−1) was tested with RDPP. The fluorescence intensity of RDPP was inversely proportional to the oxygen concentration b) RDPP fluorescence imaging at various concentrations of HMCaO2 c)RDPP was used to test the O2 production capacity of CuLCDs at different concentrations(0, 2.56, 3.84, 5.1, 6.4 and 7.7 μg mL−1) d) RDPP was used to test the O2 production capacity of CuLCDs at different pH solution e) RDPP fluorescence imaging at various concentrations of CuLCDs f) RDPP was used to test the O2 production capacity of HACOCLC at different concentrations(0,20,30,40,50 and 60 μg mL−1) g) RDPP was used as an O2 probe to compare the oxygen production capacity of HMCaO2, CuLCDs and HACOCLC. h) RDPP fluorescence imaging in HACOCLC i) Mechanism for self-supplied H2O2/O2 of HACOCLC.

Cell test results of HACOCLC: MTT assay, ROS level, GSH level, live and dead cell staining a) Cellular uptake of HACOCLC. Blue fluorescence represents the nucleus and red fluorescence represents HACOCLC entering the cells. The red fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with the number of HACOCLC b) Quantitative histogram of cells uptake fluorescence c) The ROS levels in CT26 cells were detected with DCFH-DA as ROS probe. The green fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with ROS level. d) Intracellular ROS level fluorescence quantitative histogram e) Cytotoxicity was analyzed by MTT f) Double staining of living and dead cells after treatment with HACOCLC and CuLCDs. Red fluorescence represents dead cells, green fluorescence represents living cells, and fluorescence intensity is positively correlated with cell number. g) Fluorescence quantitative histogram of live and dead cells staining h) GSH level of CT26 cells was detected by GSH kit, and the absorbance was positively correlated with the GSH concentration. The fluorescence intensity histogram was plotted by Image J analysis software. Data were presented as means ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

HACOCLC caused calcium overload in vitro a) Fluorescence imaging of Mito-Tracker Red and JC-1 assays of CT26 cells under different conditions b) Fluorescence imaging of Mito-Tracker Green and DAPI assays of CT26 cells under different conditions c) JC-1 fluorescence quantitative histogram d) Mito-Tracker Green fluorescence quantitative histogram. Quantitative histograms of fluorescence intensity were plotted using Image J analysis software. Data were presented as means ± SD (n = 3), **p < 0.01. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Self-supplied H2O2/O2 evaluation by HACOCLC In vitro a) Intracellular H2O2 amount after incubation of different concentrations of HACOCLC with CT26 b) Intracellular H2O2 content with and without illumination c) Intracellular O2 levels. RDPP is the O2 probe d) Oxygen level fluorescence quantitative histogram e) Intracellular levels of HIF-1α and PD-L1. The green fluorescence intensity is proportional to HIF-1α expression. Red fluorescence intensity was proportional to the expression of PD-L1 f) Fluorescence quantitative histogram of HIF-1α and PD-L1. The fluorescence intensity quantitative histogram was drawn by Image J analysis software. Data were presented as means±SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

In vivo distribution of HACOCLC a) In vivo fluorescence imaging b) Photothermal imaging in vivo c) Fluorescence quantitative histogram of tumor d) Quantitative histogram of tumor temperature c) drug distribution in major organs and tumors. The fluorescence intensity quantitative histogram was drawn by Image J analysis software. Data were presented as means ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Evaluation therapeutic effect of HACOCLC in vivo a) Treatment protocol for the tumor model mice b) Solid tumor imaging c) Tumor volume change curve during treatment d) Mice body weight changes curve during treatment e) H&E staining of major organs and tumor tissues after completion of treatment Data were presented as means ± SD (n = 3), **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Self-supplied H2O2/O2 evaluation by HACOCLC In vivo a) H2O2 content in CuLCDs and HACOCLC tissues under light and no light conditions b) HIF-1α fluorescence staining in tissues c) Quantitative analysis of HIF-1α fluorescence intensity in tissues, the fluorescence intensity was negatively correlated with the content d) PD-L1 fluorescence staining in tissues e) Quantitative analysis of PD-L1 fluorescence intensity in tissues, the fluorescence intensity was negatively correlated with the content. Fluorescence intensity quantification histograms were plotted using Image J analysis software Data were presented as means ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

head.ce:title: A hyaluronic acid Schiff base @ hollow calcium peroxide @ copper carbon dots nanoconjugate enhances chemodynamic tumor therapy based on multiple approaches strategy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemodynamic therapy (CDT) is a highly promising cancer treatment strategy. However, its clinical application is severely limited due to insufficient H2O2 levels. To overcome this critical issues, based on self-supply H2O2 strategy this study designed a multifunctional nano-reactor hyaluronic acid (HA)-cinnamaldehyde(CA)Schiff base(HA-CA)@hollow CaO2(HMCaO2)/Copper doped luteolin carbon dots (CuLCDs) (abbreviation:HACOCLC). HACOCLC based on self-supplied H2O2/O2 strategy to enhance the anti-tumor effect. HACOCLC preferentially enrich at the tumor site under the tumor targeting effect of HA. pH stimulation triggered the release of HMCaO2 and CuLCDs from HACOCLC. HMCaO2 hydrolyzed to produce H2O2, Ca2+ and O2, H2O2 compensated substrates for CDT, O2 down-regulates hypoxia-inducing factor (HIF-1α) and programmed death ligand 1(PD-L1) to consolidate the therapeutic effect, and Ca2+ entered mitochondria to cause calcium overload and induced cell death. CuLCDs underwent Fenton-like reaction to produce •OH (CDT effect) and O2, which further increased the O2 level. CuLCDs has near-infrared light (808 nm) thermal conversion (conversion efficiency was 40.3 %) for photothermal therapy (PTT), Cu2+ depletion of glutathione amplified the level of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and improved the therapeutic effect. HACOCLC self-supplied H2O2 and O2 to enhance CDT, and effectively inhibited tumor growth through the combined action of CDT/PTT/ calcium overload.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are important regulatory signaling molecules in cells [1,2]. Appropriate levels of ROS play a crucial role in regulating physiological phenomena such as intracellular signal transduction and gene expression [3,4]. When the level of ROS exceeds the cellular tolerance threshold, oxidative stress occurs, disrupting the adaptive mechanisms, and ROS is rapidly cleared by glutathione to maintain homeostasis [5,6]. Scientists have exploited the oxidative stress mechanisms of tumor cells to develop ROS-mediated therapies, such as chemodynamic therapy (CDT) [7–9]. CDT utilizes the Fenton reaction to catalyze the conversion of H2O2 into highly oxidative •OH, leading to lipid oxidation, protein inactivation, or DNA damage, ultimately inducing apoptosis in cancer cells [10,11]. However, although tumor microenvironment (TME) contains more H2O2 than normal cells (100 μM) [12,13], it is still insufficient for CDT requirements. How to increase the concentration of H2O2 in TME is the key to improve the effect of CDT.

Cancer cells grow uncontrollably, consuming large amounts of oxygen to produce lactic acid, leading to a local downregulation of pH and hypoxia intensification in the TME. Hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF-1α) is highly expressed in TME, promoting cancer metastasis and inducing the upregulation of programmed cell death-ligand 1 (PD-L1). HIF-1α and PD-L1 is involved in the metastasis, invasion, and angiogenesis [14]. Therefore, hypoxia has become an important target in tumor treatment. Consolidating and improving the efficacy of CDT through up-regulating O2 level may become an effective therapeutic strategy.

So far, a large number of direct and indirect methods have been developed to deliver H2O2 from external sources to the TEM [15–17], such as in polymeric nanocarriers that co-deliver H2O2 and Fe3O4 nanoparticles [18], where the polymer is destroyed under ultrasound irradiation to release separately encapsulated H2O2 and Fe3O4. In addition to the direct delivery of H2O2, metal peroxide nanomaterials also provide H2O2 in tumor regions through hydrolysis reactions [19]. Although these strategies can enhance H2O2 and O2 concentration to some extent, the supply was limited, and the preparation process of nanostructures was complex.

Calcium peroxide (CaO2) has been widely applied in the biomedical field due to its high biocompatibility [20,21]. As a “solid H2O2”, CaO2 can react with H2O to generate H2O2 and O2. CaO2 has a high O2 content, making it a promising O2 and H2O2 provider in tumor therapy [22,23]. Importantly, it has been found in recent years that Ca2+ entry into mitochondria causes calcium overload and induces apoptosis [24–26]. Compared to other peroxide nanomaterials, CaO2 possesses a triple function of generating O2, H2O2, and Ca2+ overload, giving it a greater advantage in enhancing CDT.

Iron-based nanomaterials are the most researched CDT reagents. However, Fe2+ Fenton reaction is highly dependent on relatively stringent acidic conditions (pH 2–4), and the reaction rate is low (≈63 M−1 s−1) [27], which severely limits application under in weakly acidic TME (pH 6.4–6.9) [28]. In contrast, the Cu Fenton-like reaction can occur in weakly acidic, and the reaction rate of Cu+ is about 160 times that of Fe2+ [29]. Copper based CDT agents hold great potential in cancer therapy [30,31]. Furthermore, copper-based nanomaterials have the capability to consume GSH, thereby increasing oxidative stress and enhancing antitumor effects [32,33].

Carbon dots are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, characterized by a simple preparation processes, and excellent optical activity, and are widely using in fields such as biological imaging and photothermal conversion [34,35]. Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a linear macromolecular polysaccharide [36]. HA possesses unique viscoelastic and rheological properties, playing an important physiological role in organisms [37]. CD44 is a receptor for HA, which is highly expressed on the membrane surface of many cancer cells. Various HA-based tumor-targeted nanodrug delivery systems have been developed utilizing the interaction between HA and CD44 [38,39].

In this study, based on self-supply H2O2/O2 strategy we designed a multifunctional nano-reactor HA-cinnamaldehyde (CA) Schiff base (HA-CA)@hollow CaO2(HMCaO2)/Copper doped luteolin carbon dots (CuLCDs) (abbreviation:HACOCLC). HACOCLC was composed of HA-CA, HMCaO2, and CuLCDs through co-assembly. HACOCLC had the following advantages:1 Self-supplying H2O2/ O2 to enhance CDT and downregulate the levels of HIF-1α and PD-L1; 2 Photothermal effect and PTT assisted enhancement of anti-tumor effect; 3 Ca2+ overload induced cell death and enhanced CDT through photothermal effect; 5 Consuming GSH to increase ROS levels; 6 HA-mediated visualization for targeted tumor accumulation. The anti-tumor mechanism of HACOCLC based on self-supplied H2O2/O2 and photothermal assisted enhancement of multi-mode combination is shown in Scheme 1 .

CaCl2 (97 %), NH3·H2O (25 %), H2O2 (30 %), Ethylenediamine (99 %), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopro- pyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS), Luteolin(98 %), cupric acetate (98 %), Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP), glutathione (GSH), 5,5′-dithio bis(2- nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bro-mide (MTT) were all purchased from Sahn Chemical Technology (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. 4′,6-Diamidino-2-phenyl-indole (DAPI), 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), and mouse colon cancer cell CT26 were purchased from Changsha Aibiwei Biological Technology Co. Ltd. (China). Anti-HIF-1α/AF488, Alexa Fluor 488-CRT, CD8A rabbit pAb were purchased from Immuno-Way Biotechnology Company. The ATP test kit was purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). The GSH assay kit was purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. Balb/C mice were provided by the Second Affiliated Hospital of the Harbin Medical University (China). All experiments on mice were in line with China's national standards for the care and use of laboratory animals and were approved by the Experimental Animal Ethics Committee of Harbin Normal University. A Tecnai G2 F200 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Netherlands) was used to observe the particle morphology. A 90 Plus PALS zeta potential and particle size analyzer (Brookhaven) was used for the detection of the zeta potential and particle size. A UV-2600 ultraviolet spectrophotometer was used for the detection of substance absorption (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). An F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to detect the fluorescence emission properties of substances. A Tensor II Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) was used to examine the characteristic functional groups of matter. A D5005X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Siemens, Germany) was used to investigate the crystal structures. A Leica DM IL IED fluorescent inverted microscope, a Leica HistoCore PEARL automatic tissue dehydrator, a Leica HistoCore Arcadia H paraffin-embedding machine, a Leica HistoCore AUTOCUT R Automatic rotary microtome, a Leica DM4B upright microscope, and other instruments were used for cell and histological studies. All Leica series instruments were supplied by Leica Microsystems (Shanghai, China) Trading Co. Ltd. A laser confocal microscope Leica TCS SP5 (Germany) was used to observe immunofluorescence experiments of protein molecules. The Tanon ABL X6 (China) imaging system was used for in vivo imaging.

0.735 g of CaCl2 was placed in a reaction flask, 100 mL of absolute ethanol and 1 mL of ammonia were added, and 0.5 mL of H2O2 (6.5 mmol) was added while stirring, dropwise and stirred overnight at room temperature. The reaction mixture was centrifuged for 5 min (11,000 rpm) and the pellet was collected, then washed 3 times with 10 mL of deionized water and 95 % ethanol and vacuum-dried to obtain HMCaO2.

50 mg luteolin and 100 mg HMCaO2 were added to 50 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 15 min, transferred to an autoclave, and reacted at 180 °C for 24 h. Cool naturally to room temperature, pour the reaction solution into a beaker, add 30 mg of copper acetate, stir overnight at room temperature, and centrifuge for 10 min (rpm 9000) to collect the pellet. The pellet was dialysed with deionized water for 48 h and then freeze-dried to obtain HMCaO2@CuLCDs. HMCaO2@LCDs obtained using the same method, except that copper acetate is not added.

Add 20 mg HA, 15.5 mg EDC, and 11.5 mg NHS to 10 mL of deionized water and stir at 60 °C for 6 h, add 2.7 g EDA, and stir overnight. The mixture was dialysed in deionized water (molecular weight cutoff 5000 Da) for 3 days. During dialysis, the water is changed every 6 h. Slowly drop 1 g of cinnamaldehyde into the solution with stirring for 30 min at room temperature, then slowly drop 5 mLHMCaO2@CuLCDs ethanol solution (1 mg mL−1) into the mixture and stir for 48 h at room temperature. Centrifuge the reaction mixture for 5 min (11,000 rpm) to collect the pellet. HACOCLC was washed 3 times with deionized water and 95 % ethanol and vacuum-dried.

200 μg mL−1 HACOCLC in water was placed in a 4 mL cuvette and irradiated with 808 nm lamps of different power (0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 W), and the temperature change was recorded every 30 s. Different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 μg mL−1) of HACOCLC were placed in cuvettes and irradiated with a laser (808 nm, 1.5 W) to record temperature changes. Imaging is taken using a thermal imaging camera. The photothermal conversion efficiency was evaluated by recording the temperature change of HACOCLC (200 μg mL−1) irradiated with an 808 nm laser with a power density of 1.5 W cm−2, and the photothermal conversion efficiency η was evaluated by Eq. ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 produced by HACOCLC
ce:section.ce:para: The materials to be tested are HA@CaO2@LCDs and HACOCLC. A mixed solution of 200 μL of TMB (6 mg mL−1 anhydrous ethanol solution) and 50 μL of horseradish peroxidase (0.01 mg mL−1) was added to the cuvette, stirred for 30 s, then the materials (0, 40, 80, 160, 240, 320 μL) were added, diluted to 4 mL with PBS (pH 5.5), and 10 μL of dilute hydrochloric acid was added dropwise. The reaction was terminated after 5 min, and the absorbance at 450 nm was recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Using MB as a hydroxyl radical scavenger, the •OH production capacity of HACOCLC was studied. The experiment was divided into light experiment and no light experiment. HACOCLC (0, 40, 80, 160, 240, 320 μL ethanol solution) and 200 μL MB (0.15 mg mL−1) were placed in cuvettes, respectively. Dilute the mixed solution to 4 mL with PBS (pH 5.5, 6.8, 7.4). The solution temperature was raised to 50 °C by 808 nm (1.5 W cm−2) laser irradiation and held for 1 h. Record the absorbance at 650 nm with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The lightless experiment was reacted at room temperature for 1 h, and the other experimental procedures were consistent with the light experiment.

RDPP was used as an O2 probe to study the O2 production capacity of HACOCLC. Experiments are divided into light experiments and lightless experiments. The experiment was carried out under isolated air conditions, and all reactions were discharged from the air with a stream of nitrogen. HACOCLC (0, 40, 80, 160, 240, and 320 μg mL−1) was placed in cuvettes, 100 μL of RDPP (1× 10−2 M) was added, diluted to 3.9 mL with PBS (pH 5.5, 6.8, 7.4), and 100 μL (100 mmol) of H2O2 was added. The solution temperature was raised to 50 °C by 808 nm (1.5 W cm−2) laser irradiation and held for 1 h. Fluorescence intensity (λex = 455 nm, λem = 615 nm) was detected with a fluorescence spectrophotometer, recorded every 2 min. The lightless experiment was reacted at room temperature for 1 h, and the other experimental processes were consistent with the light experiment.

DTNB was used as a GSH probe to study GSH consumption in HACOCLC. The experiment was divided into light experiment and no light experiment. Different concentrations of HACOCLC (0,10,20, 40, 50, 60 and 80 μg mL−1) were placed in cuvettes, 120 μL of GSH in water (20 μM) were added, then 3.94 mL PBS (pH 5.5 or 6.4 and 7.4) was added, was stirred for 60 min at room temperature, then 60 μL of DTNB (20 μM) was added and the co-incubation was continued for 30 min. Absorbance was recorded with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 325 nm and 412 nm. The experimental procedure in the light group was the same as above, except that HACOCLC was exposed to 808 nm laser(1.5 W cm−2) for 10 min during co-incubation with GSH.

The cells (CT26, L929) were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % antibiotics (penicillin-streptomycin) and placed in an incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. The medium was changed every 24 h. When the cell morphology was good and the bottom surface of the culture flask was 80 % overgrown, the original medium was discarded, and the cells were washed three times with 1 mL of PBS. Then, 1 mL of trypsin solution was added to digest the cells. Once the cells became spherical, 1 mL of medium was added to terminate digestion. The cells were centrifuged using a low-speed centrifuge (1000 rpm for 5 min), the cell pellet was collected, and medium was added for subculture at a ratio of 1:2.

CT26 cells were seeded in 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator. Discard the medium and wash 3 times with PBS. Add 1 mL of different nanomaterials (CuLCDs, HACOCLC) at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200 μg mL−1) and incubate in a 5 % CO2 incubator for 8 h. The other set was incubated with 200 μg mL−1 for 4 h, irradiated with 808 nm and 50 mW cm−2 light for 5 min, and then continued for 4 h. Aspirate the material and wash 3 times with PBS. Add 1 mL of the ROS probe DCFH-DA (50 μg mL−1) to each well and continue incubating for 30 min, then observe under a fluorescence inverted microscope and complete taking pictures.

Add 1 mL of HACOCLC (0, 50, 100, 200 μg mL−1) to cell culture bottles with well-formed cell morphology. The CT26 cells were divided into light group and non-light group. The non-light group was incubated for 24 h. The light group was incubated in the dark for 4 h, followed by irradiation with 808 nm light (50 mW cm−2) for 5 min, and then continues to incubate for 20 h. Collect the media containing substances from the respective cell culture bottles and the cell suspension made from digested cells, mix the two, and centrifuge to collect the cells. Washed twice with PBS, added 0.5 mL of PBS to resuspend the cells, and sonicated in an ice water bath to disrupt the cells (power 300 W, 3–5 s each time, repeat 3–5 times with a 30 s interval). Taken the disrupted homogenate and measure the GSH content in the cells according to the kit instructions.

CT26 cells were seeded on 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator. Discard the medium and wash 3 times with PBS. 1 mL of CuLCDs and HACOCLC (0, 150, 200 μg mL−1) were added and incubated for 8 h. Cells were washed 3 times with PBS, then stained with 50 μL of RDPP (10 μg mL−1) for 10 min, washed 3 times with PBS, observed and photographed under a fluorescent inverted microscope. Light experiments were performed using 200 μg mL−1 of HACOCLC samples. HACOCLC and CT26 cells were incubated for 4 h, irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 5 min, and then continued for 4 h. Other experimental procedures are the same as those in non-optical experiments.

CT26 cells were seeded on 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator. The experiments were divided into two groups: shallow group and no shallow group. Add 1 mL of HACOCLC (0, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg mL−1) to the cell culture flask and incubate for 4 h. The light group was irradiated for 5 min, and then incubated for 4 h. The H2O2 content in CT26 cells was measured according to the procedure of the H2O2 detection kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test of intracellular HIF-1α and PD-L1 levels
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments were divided into two groups: the shallow group and the non-shallow group. CT26 cells were seeded in a 6-well plate and incubated for 24 h in a 37 °C, 5 % CO2 incubator, after which the medium was discarded. Wash three times with PBS. The CT26 cells were cultured with 1 mL (0, 150, 200 μg mL−1, 200 μg mL−1 + 808 nm) HACOCLC for 4 h. Aspirate the material and wash three times with PBS. Then, stain with 100 μL of anti-HIF-1α/AF488 (HIF-1α chromogen) or AF488 (PD-L1 chromogen) for 40 min, fix the cells with 1 mL of 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for 10 min, wash with PBS, stain with 1 mL of DAPI (2 μg mL−1) for 10 min, discard the medium, wash three times with PBS, and finally observe and photograph the cells under a fluorescence inverted microscope.

Select 7-week-old, 18–20 g female Balb/c mice as experimental subjects, and choose CT26 cells as the cell line. CT26 cells (100 μL, approximately 1 million cells) are subcutaneously inoculated into the right hip of the mice. When the tumor volume reaches 100 mm3, HACOCLC is administered via the tail vein at a dosage of 10 mg kg−1. After administration, observations are made at different time points using an in vivo fluorescence imager and an infrared thermal imager. Tumor tissues from the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys are obtained based on the peak time of tumor accumulation shown in imaging, and fluorescence imaging is used to observe the accumulation of the drug in the organs and tumors.

Seven-week-old female Balb/c mice were selected as model animals, inoculated subcutaneously with CT26 cells, and randomly divided into 5 groups (5 in each group) when the tumor volume was about 100 mm3: Saline group, CaO2 group, CuLCDs group, HACOCLC group, and HACOCLC + Light group. Treatment is administered on days 0, 5 and 10, followed by light 6 h post-dose and the end of treatment on day 14. HACOCLC was administered via the tail vein at a dose of 10 mg kg−1 and irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 10 min. The body weight and tumor volume of the mice are weighed and measured at regular intervals.

After 14 days of treatment, euthanize the mice in each group, collect the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) and solid tumors from each group, weigh them, and take photographs. Perform immunofluorescence staining to analyze the complete tumor tissue. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining will be conducted to analyze the potential toxicity of the nanodrug on tumors and major organs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection experiment of intratissue H2O2 levels
ce:section.ce:para: Tumor tissue is removed from the mouse, and after cell counting, a 1 × 106 single-cell suspension is prepared. H2O2 content in tumor tissue was measured according to the procedure of the H2O2 assay kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assays of HIF-1α and PD-L1 in tumor tissue
ce:section.ce:para: After 14 days of treatment, the tumor tissues from different treatment groups were formed into paraffin sections, followed by deparaffinization, antigen retrieval, fixation, and sealing. According to the kit instructions, the tumor tissues were incubated with primary and secondary antibodies, then stained with immunofluorescence and analyzed using a fluorescence inverted microscope.

All experiments were repeated >3 times. All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. One-way ANOVA was performed to compare statistical analyses of GraphPad Prism 8.0. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

The preparation process of HACOCLC was shown in Scheme 2 . HA was activated with EDC/NHS, aminated with an excess of ethylenediamine, purified using a dialysis bag, and reacted with CA to produce HA-CA. HMCaO2 was synthesized according to the method provided in reference [40, 41]. CaCl2 was dispersed in methanol, a small amount of ammonia and H2O2 were added, and stirred at room temperature to obtain HMCaO2. HMCaO2@CuLCDs was synthesized in situ using a hydrothermal method. Luteolin and HMCaO2 were dispersed in water and added to a high-pressure reactor to react at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling, copper acetate was added and stirred overnight to obtain HMCaO2@CuLCDs. HA-CA and HMCaO2@CuLCDs co-assemble to form HACOCLC at room temperature by hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction.

The morphology of the nanoparticles was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 1a showed that HMCaO2 was a hollow sphere with particle size ranging from 75 nm to 125 nm. X-Ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 1b) showed that HMCaO2 had diffraction peaks at 2θ = 20°, 30°, 35°, 40°, and 45°, corresponding to (002), (110), (112), (103), and (202) crystal planes, respectively, consistent with the standard card for CaO2 (PDF# 03–0865). Fig. 1c showed the nitrogen adsorption-desorption curve for HMCaO2, which had typical properties for mesoporous materials with pore sizes ranging from 5 nm to 15 nm. Fig. 1d was a TEM of HMCaO2@CuLCDs, which showed that was a hollow sphere with a large number of black particles inside the hollow sphere. The black particle shown clear lattice stripes in HRTEM, and the fringe spacing d = 0.21 nm, it was consistent with the graphite crystal plane (100) [42], which demonstrated the in-situ formation of carbon dots on HMCaO2. Fig. 1e was the nitrogen adsorption-desorption curve for HMCaO2@CuLCDs, maintaining typical mesoporous material properties with a pore size distribution between 2 and 7 nm and a smaller pore size compared to HMCaO2, which was attribute to the smaller pore size due to the in-situ carbon dots formation, which demonstrated that the carbon dots are successfully generated in situ in the pore channels of HMCaO2. XRD (Fig. S1) showed HMCaO2@CuLCDs with a clear HMCaO2 characteristic peak. Fig. 1f was a HMCaO2@CuLCDs Raman spectrum showing typical D and G peaks at 1370 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 for graphitized carbon dots, and vibrational peaks for CaO2 at 840 cm−1. As shown in Fig. 1g, HACOCLC had a regular spherical morphology with a particle size distribution between 100 and 200 nm. The diffraction peaks of CaO2 were faintly visible in the XRD spectrum of HACOCLC, which was overlaid by HA-CA in the outer layer (Fig. S1), the D and G peaks of the CuLCDs were found in Fig. S2. Fig. 1h showed the Fourier transform infrared vibration spectrum (FTIR), a CaO vibration peak of CaO2 at 870 cm−1, which was seen in both HMCaO2@CuLCDs and HACOCLC. The blunt peaks between 3200 and 3550 nm belong to the -OH or -NH2 vibration peaks, confirming that there were a large number of -OH or -NH2 groups on the surface of HMCaO2, CuLCDs and HMCaO2@CuLCDs, 1650 cm−1 was attributed to CN vibration, indicated the presence of Schiff base bonds in HACOCLC, these results further confirmed the successful preparation of HACOCLC. Fig. 1i showed the Zeta potential map. HMCaO2 had the Zeta potential of +22.3 mV, and HMCaO2@CuLCDs had the Zeta potential of the +10.2 mV, indicated that the Zeta potential was moving to the negative side after the HMCaO2 loading CuLCDs, and indicated surface of the CuCDs contains the negative charge -OH group, which was the same as the -OH peak shows in the FTIR. HA-CA had a negative Zeta potential (−14.5 mV). Negatively charged HACOCLC (−10.3 mV) was obtained after modification of HMCaO2@CuLCDs by HA-CA. FTIR and Zeta potential confirmed that HA-CA and HMCaO2@CuLCDs were co-assembled into HACOCLC by hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction. Fig. 1j showed the electron diffraction spectra (XPS) of HACOCLC, peaks at 930 eV, 530 eV, 400 eV, 350 eV, 285 eV, were assigned to Cu2p, O1s, N1s, Ca2p, and C1s with percentages of 1.37 %, 22.33 %, 12.7 %, 18.47 %, respectively. The loading rates of CuLCDs and HACaO2 were 12.8 % and 35.6 %, respectively. Fig. 1k showed the XPS of Ca2p [43], where the 350.9 eV peak belonged to Ca(II)2p1/2 and the 347.4 eV belonged to Ca(II)2p3/2. Fig. 1l showed the XPS for Cu2p [44], where the 953.3 eV peak belonged to Cu(II)2p1/2 and 932.7 eV peak belonged to Cu(II)2p3/2. Note that 955.5 eV belonged to Cu(I)2p1/2 and 934.7 eV belonged to Cu(I)2p3/2, suggested that a portion of Cu2+ was reduced to Cu+ during in-situ carbon dot formation, which may be due to the reducibility of carbon dots. Fig. S3 showed the good stability of HACOCLC, there were absorbance, zeta potential, and DLS size remaining largely unchanged in saline, PBS (pH 7.4), and RMPI1640 (10 % FBS) media.

HA and CA construct Schiff base (HA-CA) through ethylenediamine. As everyone knows, the Schiff base is pH sensitive, and the Schiff base bond can be disrupted under acidic conditions. Therefore, when Schiff base (HA-CA) was decomposed by acid, the HA-CA layer fell off exposed HMCaO2@CuLCDs, which leaded to the hydrolysis of CaO2 nanospheres and the release of CuLCDs. We studied the morphological changes of HACOCLC at pH 5.5, 6.4 and 7.4. The results are showed in Fig. 2a. After soaking in pH 7.4 PBS for 8 h, the spherical edge profile was clear, and the morphology remained unchanged. After soaking in pH 6.4 PBS for 3 h, the edge structure of the nanoparticles became loose and expanded outward, and the morphology changed from spherical to elliptical. After continuous soaking for 8 h, the nanoparticles degraded into multiple pieces, released the encapsulated CuLCDs. After soaking in pH 5.5 PBS for 1 h, HACOCLC was completely exposed HMCaO2@CuCDs and clear HMCaO2 nanostructure could be seen. After 3 h, the HMCaO2 nanostructures were seriously damaged, and after 8 h, only some black particles (carbon dots) remained. The change of HACOCLC morphology could also be seen from the change of Zeta potential (Fig. S4). When impregnated in pH 7.4 PBS for 1 h, Zeta potential remained unchanged, while when impregnated in pH 6.4 PBS for 1 h, Zeta potential was −1.9 mV. HACOCLC was impregnated in pH 5.5 PBS for 1 h, and the Zeta potential was +13.5 mV, which was corrected compared with HMCaO2@CuLCDs, indicated that HA-CA was completely shed and some CuLCDs were released. The above experiments confirmed the pH-sensitive degradation and release characteristics of HACOCLC for CuLCDs. Fig. 2b showed the cumulative release rate of CuLCDs in HACOCLC under simulated TME (pH 5.5, 6.4) and normal physiological conditions (pH 7.4). The results indicated that the cumulative release amount at pH 7.4 after 12 h was 8.7 %, at pH 6.4 was 76 %, and at pH 5.5 was 100 %. Notably, in the pH 5.5 solution, the release volume surged within 4 h and then slowed down, indicated that the release of HACOCLC was pH-dependent, demonstrated excellent release functionality with TME specificity, avoiding damage to normal tissues.

Absorption of CuLCDs came across the entire UV–Vis-NIR region (Fig. 2c). HMCaO2@CuLCDs and HACOCLC were similar to CuLCDs but are stronger than CuLCDs in the UV–Vis region, but attenuate in the NIR region. HACOCLC had a strong flat peak at 430 nm and the bottom edge extended to 900 nm. Under laser excitation at 350 nm, HACOCLC had an emission peak at 452 nm with the basal edge of the peak extending into the red region. The absorption characteristics of HACOCLC in the near-infrared region (NIR) indicated its possible NIR photothermal conversion capacity [45]. Therefore, we investigated the photothermal conversion capacity of HACOCLC at different concentrations and light sources. The results are shown in Fig. 2e and f. The results showed that when irradiated with an 808 nm laser (1.5 W cm−2), the solution temperature increased significantly with increasing dose and light time, and at a concentration of 400 μg cm−1, the temperature increased to 55 °C, while the temperature in the control group changed only slightly. At 200 μg cm−1, the temperature rises to 48 °C. The temperature of HACOCLC (50 μg cm−1) significantly increases with the increase in light source power (0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 W cm−2) and the duration time of illumination. Fig. 2g showed the heating-cooling curve controlled by the light source switch. During the illumination process, the temperature rose rapidly, and after the removal of the light source, the temperature dropped back to the initial level, indicated that the photothermal conversion function of HACOCLC was relate to the control of the light source switch. Fig. 2h presented the fifth photothermal conversion cycle curve controlled by the light source switch, where the maximum temperature of each cycle remained constant, demonstrated that HACOCLC possessed strong resistance to photobleaching and stable photothermal conversion performance. Fig. 3i illustrated the linear curve of heat transfer constant-illumination time (−Lnθ-Time), indicated a good linear relationship between heating efficiency and illumination time, with the linear equation y = 204.54038x + 44.901 and a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.990. According to the method in the literature [46], the photothermal conversion efficiency of the 808 nm laser is 40.3 %. Fig. 3j showed the photothermal imaging of HACOCLC samples at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg cm−1) (808 nm, 1.5 W cm−2, 10 min). Considering that temperatures above 50 °C can denature proteins leading to cell death, to avoid burns in both in vivo and in vitro experiments, the light source power used in the optical experiments was set at 1.5 W cm−2, the maximum concentration of HACOCLC was 200 μg cm−1.

Cu2+ is an excellent GSH consumer [47], so HACOCLC may have a GSH depletion capacity. In this study, 5,5′-dithio-bis-nitrobenzoic acid (DTNB) was used to study the ability of HACOCLC to consume GSH. The absorption peak of DTNB at 325 nm was reduced by GSH to form 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoic acid (TNB), and the absorption at 325 nm of DTNB was positively correlated with the ability of HACOCLC to consume GSH. After co-incubation of HACOCLC (0,10,20,40,50,60 and 80 μg mL−1) with GSH for 60 min, then DTNB was added to continue the incubation for 30 min, and the absorbance at 325 nm and 412 nm was recorded. As shown in Fig. 3a and b, the results shown that at 325 nm, the absorbance increased gradually with increasing HACOCLC dose. The 80 μg mL−1 sample consumed 89.5 % GSH, and HACOCLC showed excellent GSH depletion ability, compared with a GSH depletion rate of 96 % after 10 min of 808 nm laser (1.5 W cm−2) irradiation, which was 6.5 % higher than that in the no-light group, confirming that photothermal effect promoted the depletion GSH reaction of HACOCLC. The study also found that under acidic conditions, the ability to consume GSH was further enhanced (Fig. 3b). In HACOCLC (40 μg mL−1) solutions at pH 5.5, 82 % of GSH was depleted, compared to 77.3 % and 70.1 % in pH 6.4 and 7.4 solutions, respectively, confirming that acidity promoted the HACOCLC GSH consuming reaction. Therefore, HACOCLC was an excellent GSH depleting agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HACOCLC self-supplied to H2O2 and O2
ce:section.ce:para: Under weakly acidic conditions, the surface modified HA-CA of HACOCLC decompositions to expose HMCaO2@CuLCDs, and the HMCaO2 shell was hydrolyzed to release Cu2+ and LCDs. The hydrolysis reaction of CaO2 and Fenton-like reaction of Cu2+ generated H2O2 and O2. Firstly, the H2O2 self-supplying capacity of HACOCLC was investigated. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were used to study the ability to produce H2O2 [48,49]. The principle was that HRP catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 to produce •OH, and TMB was oxidized by •OH to produce Oxy-TMB. The absorbance of Oxy-TMB at 450 nm was proportional to the amount of H2O2. HA@HMCaO2@LCDs's H2O2 production capacity was investigated. HA@HMCaO2@LCDs sample (60 μgmL−1) was incubated with HRP in an acidic (pH 5.5) solution for 100 min, TMB was then added, and the color of the sample changed from colorless to blue and finally to yellow (Fig. 3c). The color rendering principle of H2O2 detection by HRP-TMB was shown in Fig. 3d, HRP decomposed H2O2 to produce •OH, which oxidizes TMB to quinone TMB (Oxy-TMB). The Oxy-TMB and TMB form blue dimeric intermediate, which was continued to oxidize all into Oxy-TMB, and the solution become yellow (Fig. 3d). The different concentrations HA@HMCaO2@LCDs(0, 10, 20, 40, 60 and 80 μg mL−1) were incubated with HRP in an acidic (pH 5.5) solution for 100 min followed by TMB for 5 min. The absorbance of Oxy-TMB at 450 nm was recorded with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Fig. 3e), The absorbance of the control group was zero, indicating that H2O2 was not generated in the absence of HA@HMCaO2@LCDs. With the increased of HA@HMCaO2@LCDs concentration, the absorbance of Oxy-TMB increased, confirming the production of H2O2. H2O2 was quantified using the hydrogen peroxide quantitative detection kit and Oxy-TMB absorption standard curve (Fig. S5). The results were shown in Fig. 3f, HA@HMCaO2@LCDs produced H2O2 in a dose-dependent manner. HA@HMCaO2@LCDs (pH 5.5) hydrolysis reaction to produce H2O2 yield gradually increased with reaction time, H2O2 yield reached 91 % after 60 min (Fig. 3g), showed excellent H2O2 production ability. Next, the H2O2 production capacity of HACOCLC was investigated. Co-incubate HACOCLC (0, 10, 20, 40, 60 and 80 μg mL−1, pH 5.5), HRP and TMB in purified water for 100 min, the absorbance of Oxy-TMB was shown in Fig. S6. The absorbance of Oxy-TMB increased with the concentration of HACOCLC, which proved that H2O2 was produced concentration-dependent (Fig. 3h). By comparing the H2O2 production of HACOCLC and HA@HMCaO2@LCDs (Table S1), it was found that the H2O2 production of HACOCLC was slightly lower than that of HA@HMCaO2@LCDs, which was because part of H2O2 was consumed by Cu-Fenton-like reaction.

We used the MB capture •OH results to continue to evaluate H2O2 production capacity of HACOCLC [50]. The principle of using MB evaluation was that H2O2 produced by hydrolysis of HACOCLC was catalyzed by Cu2+ to generate •OH, while •OH degraded MB, and the degradation rate of MB was proportional to the amount of •OH. HACOCLC and MB were co-incubated together in water, and the results were shown in Fig. 3i and j. The results shown that the concentration of HACOCLC was proportional to the degradation rate of MB, and the degradation rate of MB was the highest in acidic solution, which confirmed that HACOCLC could produce H2O2 in a concentration dependent manner, and in an acid dependent manner. It was confirmed that HACOCLC had the properties of hydrogen peroxide nanozymes based on Fenton reaction similar to HRP. In summary, the above results indicated that HACOCLC was a good H2O2 supply reagent, and it also proved that HACOCLC had similar properties to HRP and can be used as hydrogen peroxide nanozymes.

Here, we explored the self-supplying oxygen capacity of HACOCLC. Tris (4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline) ruthenium (II) dichloride, abbreviated as [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2, commercially known as RDPP. RDPP is a light-emitting probe (excitation wavelength λex 455 nm, emission wavelength λem 613 nm) commonly used for the detection and quantification of oxygen. Due to dynamic quenching, the light of RDPP is significantly reduced by molecular oxygen, therefore, molecular oxygen is detected by measuring the light intensity or decay time [51]. The oxygen concentration is inversely proportional to the red fluorescence intensity of RDPP. HMCaO2 was an oxygen generator, which decomposed in water to produce O2. Copper ion was also an oxygen generator, which produced O2 through Fenton-like reaction. Therefore, we investigated the oxygen production capacity of HMCaO2 (only CaO2 hydrolysis), CuLCDs (only Cu-Fenton reaction) and HACOCLC (both CaO2 and Cu2+), respectively, under the condition of air isolation. The concentration gradients of HACOCLC were 0, 20,30,40,50, 60 μg mL−1. The concentration gradients of HMCaO2 corresponding to the loading rate were 0, 0.71, 10.7, 14.1, 17.8, 21.4 μg mL−1, the corresponding CuLCDs concentration gradients were 0, 2.56, 3.84, 5.1, 6.4, 7.7 μg mL−1. RDPP and HMCaO2 were incubated together in water, and then the red fluorescence intensity was recorded by fluorescence spectrometer. The experimental results were shown in Fig. 4a. With the increased of HMCaO2 concentration and reaction time, the fluorescence intensity of RDPP gradually decreased, indicating that O2 was produced, and the oxygen production was proportional to the dose and reaction time. The fluorescence quenching rate of RDPP was 47.3 % within 15 min. HMCaO2 exhibited excellent potential for in situ oxygenation based on the hydrolysis reaction of CaO2. Fig. 4b showed the fluorescence imaging of RDPP after incubation in different concentrations of HMCaO2 for 15 min. It showed that the red fluorescence gradually quenching with the increase of concentration, which intuitively proved that the oxygen production gradually increases. RDPP, CuLCDs and H2O2 were co-incubated, and H2O2 negative control group and CuLCDs negative control group were set. The results were shown in Fig. 4c. The results showed that the fluorescence of RDPP was gradually quenched with the increase of CuLCDs concentration, indicating that oxygen was produced. No H2O2 was produced in the negative control group, which proved that CuLCDs oxygen production was based on the decomposition of H2O2 by the Fenton-like reaction of Cu2+. The fluorescence quenching rate of RDPP within 15 min was 38 %, and was 42.0 % in the laser irradiation group, which proved that the photothermal effect improved the oxygen production efficiency. It is well known that Fenton reaction depends on pH. Therefore, RDPP quenching under different pH (7.4, 6.4, 5.5) conditions was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4d, the higher the acidity, the higher the oxygen production efficiency, indicating that the acidity promoted Fenton-like reaction, which was consistent with the literature [52]. Fig. 4e shown the fluorescence imaging of RDPP in CuLCDs at different concentrations. The red fluorescence quenching trend shown a consistent trend of fluorescence intensity weakening (Fig. 4c). From the above results, it can be seen that the hydrolysis reaction of HMCaO2 based on CaO2 produced oxygen, and CuLCDs generated O2 based on Cu-Fenton-like reaction. We concluded that HACOCLC had stronger oxygen production capacity based on the combination of CaO2 and CuLCDs. Since HA-CA Schiff base was coated on the surface of HACOCLC, in order to accelerate the degradation rate of HACOCLC, the pH of the reaction system was adjusted to 1. HACOCLC was incubated with RDPP in water, and the changes in the fluorescence intensity of RDPP were shown in Fig. 4f. HACOCLC showed the strongest ability to produce O2, and the fluorescence quenching rate of RDPP was 82.5 % within 15 min, which was similar to the sum of HMCaO2 and CuLCDs quenching rates(85.3 %). It was confirmed that the oxygen production capacity of HACOCLC was the result of CaO2 hydrolysis and H2O2 decomposition. Fig. 4g shown that HACOCLC had the highest oxygen production capacity at the same concentration (HACOCLC 40 μg mL−1). Fig. 4h shown the fluorescence imaging of RDPP at different concentrations of HACOCLC. The red fluorescence intensity decreased with the dose of HACOCLC. HACOCLC demonstrated excellent in situ oxygen generation potential. In summary, HACOCLC had excellent H2O2 and O2 production capacity based on CaO2 hydrolysis reaction, and Cu-Fenton-like reaction further improved the oxygen production capacity. The mechanism of HACOCLC's self-supplying H2O2/O2 properties was shown in Fig. 4i. Under acidic conditions, HA-CA was destroyed and exposed HMCaO2@CuLCDs. Hydrolysis of CaO2 destroyed HMCaO2 nanostructure and released CuLCDs. CaO2 hydrolyzed to H2O2, and H2O2 further decomposed to O2, Cu-Fenton-like reaction catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 to O2.

Firstly, we evaluated the biocompatibility of HACOCLC using the MTT method. When the concentration of HACOCLC increased to 200 μg mL−1, there was no significant change in the activity of L929 cells compared to the control group (Fig. S7), indicating good biocompatibility in healthy tissues. To ensure the biocompatibility and biosafety of HACOCLC, we conducted a preliminary in vitro blood compatibility analysis using a hemolysis test. The results showed that even at a high concentration (200 μg mL−1), the hemolysis rate of HACOCLC was only 3.1 % (Fig. S8).

The CT26 cells were divided into two groups. One group were incubated with HACOCLC for 0–3 h, while the other group were incubated with HA for 1 h, followed by incubation with HACOCLC for 0–3 h. The cell nuclei were stained with DAPI, and HACOCLC appeared red under fluorescence microscopy, with the intensity of red fluorescence positively correlating with cellular uptake. Fig. 5a showed that the intracellular red fluorescence of HACOCLC gradually increased with the incubation time. After 1 h of incubation, the HACOCLC cells exhibited significant red fluorescence, with extensive red fluorescence observed after 2 h, and stronger fluorescence after 3 h, demonstrating that HACOCLC was readily taken up by the cells and that cellular uptake was time-dependent. In contrast, cellular uptake was significantly reduced after HA pretreatment, with only a small amount of HACOCLC entering the cells after 3 h of incubation. Fig. 5b showed a quantitative histogram of intracellular red fluorescence. The results indicated that after 3 h of incubation, the intracellular red fluorescence intensity in the HA-free group was 3.4 times that of the pretreatment group, suggesting that under HA mediation, HACOCLC was preferentially taken up by CT26 cells. CT26 cells are myeloid tumor cells with high expression of CD44 on the surface, and the interaction between CD44 and HA taken HACOCLC easier to enter the cells. After pretreating CT26 cells with HA, the binding of CD44 to HA results in the loss of its inductive effect on HACOCLC, thereby blocking the uptake of HACOCLC by CT26 cells [53].

ROS levels in CT26 cells were investigated using the ROS probe 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluoresceindiacetate (DCFH-DA). DCFH-DA is a generic oxidative stress indicator, enter the cell by cell ester hydrolysis enzyme generate 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrogen fluorescein (DCFH), they are then rapidly oxidized to produce fluorescein products 2′,7 ‘-dichlorofluorescein (DCF), and the fluorescence intensity is positively correlated with ROS levels [54]. After co-incubation of CT26 cells with HACOCLC and CuLCDs, respectively, intracellular ROS levels were shown in Fig. 5c. It was found that the fluorescence intensity was stronger in the HACOCLC group at the same dose. Fig. 5d shown a fluorescence quantification histogram of intracellular ROS levels. At a dose of 200 μg mL−1, ROS levels were 1.34-fold higher in the HACOCLC group than in the CuLCDs group. The results showed that the Fenton-like reaction of Cu2+ occurred intracellularly and produced •OH. Because the Fenton-like reaction depended on the concentration of H2O2, higher concentrations of H2O2 produced more •OH. HACOCLC released CaO2 in response to intracellular pH stimulation. The hydrolysis of CaO2 to produce H2O2 increased intracellular H2O2 concentration and produced more •OH. It should also be noted that ROS levels were increased under 808 nm laser irradiation, with ROS levels 30 % higher in the light group than in the no light group at 200 μg mL−1, indicating that the photothermal effect enhanced the Fenton-like reaction.

The cytotoxicity was determined through MTT analysis. CT26 cells were incubated with CuLCDs and HACOCLC at the same dosage (CuLCDs content) for 3 h, respectively. All cells were divided into light and dark groups, with a light source of 808 nm (50 W cm−2). As shown in Fig. 5e, the cell viability in all treatment groups (light and dark) gradually decreased with increasing dosage, and at the same dosage cell viability in the light group was lower than that in the dark group, for instance, at a dosage of 200 μg mL−1 the cell viability in the dark group was 22 %, while that in the light group was only 6 %. Notably, under the same conditions, the cell viability of HACOCLC was lower than that of CuLCDs, indicating that the former had stronger cytotoxicity. This result was consistent with the experimental results of intracellular ROS levels (Fig. 5c, d), confirming that this was due to the hydrolysis of CaO2 producing H2O2, which compensated for more H2O2 used in the Cu2+ Fenton-like reaction. Fig. 5f, g showed the results of the live/dead cell double staining experiment, where dead cells emit red fluorescence and live cells emit green fluorescence, these experimental results corresponded to the MTT analysis and ROS analysis. It should also be noted that at the same dosage, the levels of ROS and the number of dead cells in the light group were both higher than those in the dark group, that fully demonstrating the advantages of photothermal synergy, where the synergistic effect of CDT/PTT enhanced the inhibitory effect against tumor cells.

GSH levels in HACOCLC-treated CT26 cells were detected with a GSH assay kit. CT26 cells were incubated with different concentrations of HACOCLC (0, 50, 100, and 200 μg mL−1) and the cells were divided into light and dark groups. The light group was irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 5 min. The results shown the GSH levels in Fig. 5h. As HACOCLC concentrations increased, GSH levels were significantly downregulated. At the same concentration, the GSH levels in the light group were lower than those in the dark group. For example, the light group at a concentration of 200 μg mL−1 had a 78 % downgrade in GSH levels, while the dark group had a 55 % downgrade in GSH levels, it was confirm that the photothermal effect enhanced the consumption of GSH by 23 %. HACOCLC exhibited excellent ability to deplete GSH with the assistance of light and heat.

It has been reported that excessive Ca2+ influx into mitochondria disrupts their Ca2+ homeostasis, leading to mitochondrial Ca2+ overload and subsequent apoptosis [55]. Therefore, this study utilized commercially available JC-1 and Mito-Tracker dyes to detect changes in mitochondrial membrane potential as indicators of mitochondrial damage assessed through increased Ca2+ [56]. The JC-1 probe self-assembles into the normal mitochondrial matrix and emits red fluorescence, indicating a high membrane potential. However, when the mitochondrial membrane is damaged and the membrane potential is low, the JC-1 dye cannot self-assemble in the mitochondrial matrix. In other words, if the mitochondrial membrane is compromised and JC-1 exists in its monomeric form, it emits green fluorescence. Compared to the control group, CT26 cells treated with CaO2 exhibited significantly increased green fluorescence, confirming that CaO2-induced Ca2+ release leads to mitochondrial membrane potential disruption and mitochondrial dysfunction (Fig. 6a). CT26 cells treated with CuLCDs displayed clear green fluorescence, confirming that the Fenton-like reaction of Cu2+ induces mitochondrial membrane potential disruption and mitochondrial dysfunction. CT26 cells treated with HACOCLC showed higher levels of green fluorescence intensity than those treated with CaO2 and CuLCDs, indicating severe mitochondrial damage. The severe mitochondrial damage caused by HACOCLC was attributed to the combined effects of CaO2 and CuLCDs. Following the introduction of CuLCDs, intracellular ROS levels and oxidative stress levels increased (Fig. 5c, e, f), further exacerbating mitochondrial damage. The mitochondrial damage in the HACOCLC+Laser group was the most severe, confirming the additional damage to mitochondria caused by photothermal heating effects. Fig. 6b presented the fluorescence quantification histograms of JC-1. Furthermore, we also monitored changes in mitochondrial membrane potential using Mito-Tracker Green. The intensity of green fluorescence of Mito-Tracker Green is proportional to the mitochondrial membrane potential. Compared with the control group, the green fluorescence of the HACOCLC+Laser group was almost completely quenched, indicating that serious mitochondrial membrane damage resulted in the disappearance of membrane potential (Fig. 6c). Fig. 6d presented the fluorescence quantification histograms of Mito-Tracker dyes. The consistent experimental results from JC-1 and Mito-Tracker dyes further demonstrated that HACOCLC induced calcium overload-mediated apoptosis, with NIR photothermal assistance enhancing calcium overload.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular self-supply of H2O2/O2 and down-regulation of HIF-1α/PD-L1
ce:section.ce:para: The H2O2 assay kit (Beyotime Biotech Inc.) was used to study changes in H2O2 levels in cells. Considering that the Cu2+ Fenton-like reaction consumes H2O2, in order to eliminate the interference of Cu2+, we first studied HA@HMCaO2@LCDs. Different concentrations of HA@HMCaO2@LCDs (0, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg mL−1) were incubated with CT26 cells for 2 h, and the light group was irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 5 min. H2O2 levels in cells are shown in Fig. 7a. We found that intracellular H2O2 levels were positively correlated with HA@HMCaO2@LCDs concentrations, increasing 0.5-fold at 50 μg mL−1, 2.2-fold at 200 μg mL−1 in the dark group, and 3.3-fold in the light group (200 μg mL−1), indicating that HA@HMCaO2@LCDs had an excellent ability to increase H2O2 in situ. Next, the effect of HACOCLC on intracellular H2O2 was investigated. CT26 cells were incubated with different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200 μg mL−1) of HACOCLC and the light group was irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 5 min. The results were shown in Fig. 7b. Both the light and dark groups significantly increased H2O2 levels with increasing HACOCLC concentrations, while the light group had higher H2O2 levels. For example, at a concentration of 200 μg mL−1, the dark group had 2.5-fold higher H2O2 levels than the control group and 2.8 times higher than the light group. Although H2O2 levels in HACOCLC are slightly lower than those in HA@HMCaO2 @lcd, part of H2O2 is consumed in the Cu2+ Fenton-like reaction of HACOCLC. However, in the end, the level of H2O2 in the cell is still greatly improved, showing excellent self-supplying H2O2 ability, indicating that the hydrolysis rate of CaO2 is higher than the consumption rate of H2O2 in Fenton reaction.

Intracellular O2 levels in HACOCLC and CuLCDs after co-incubation with CT26 were investigated using RDPP as a fluorescent probe, respectively. Intracellular red fluorescence intensity was inversely correlated with O2 concentration, as shown in Fig. 7c. It was found that the O2 content increased significantly with increasing concentration, and the O2 level was higher in the light group at the same concentration of HACOCLC or CuLCDs. Fig. 7d shown a fluorescence quantification histogram of RDPP. At a concentration of 200 μg mL−1, the O2 content was 45 % higher in the dark HACOCLC group than in the CuLCDs dark group, 53 % higher in the light HACOCLC group than in the CuLCDs light group, and 8 % higher in the light group than in the dark group. HACOCLC had a higher O2 level compared to CuLCDs, thanks to CaO2 hydrolysis and the occurrence of Cu2+ Fenton-like reactions, while the latter only produced Cu2+ Fenton-like reactions. HACOCLC exhibited excellent O2 self-supply ability in both light and without light conditions, especially under light conditions, where the self-O2 self-supply ability was further improved.

Encouraged by HACOCLC's excellent self-O2 capacity, we detect intracellular HIF-1α and PD-L1 expression by immunofluorescence. CT26 cells were incubated with HACOCLC (150, 200 μg mL−1) and the light set was irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 5 min, were shown in Fig. 7e. It was found that with the increased of intracellular HACOCLC concentration, O2 levels increased and the expression of HIF-1α and PD-L1 was downregulated. The 200 μg mL−1 + Laser group had the highest intracellular O2 levels and the corresponding lowest HIF-1α and PD-L1 expressions. Fig. 7f was a histogram of fluorescence intensity for HIF-1α and PD-L1. At a concentration of 200 μg mL−1, HIF-1α levels were reduced by 60 % and PD-L1 levels were reduced by 78 % in the dark group compared to the control group. The light group had an 80 % reduction in HIF-1α levels and a 90 % reduction in PD-L1 levels compared to the control group. The results showed that HACOCLC upregulated intracellular O2 levels and downregulated HIF-1α and PD-L1 expression through self-supply of O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging and drug distribution
ce:section.ce:para: Using 7-week-old female Balb/c mice as model animals, the distribution of HACOCLC in tissues and organs in vivo was studied through fluorescence imaging and photothermal imaging. After subcutaneously inoculating CT26 cells in the back of the mice, a dose of 6 mg kg−1 was administered via the tail vein when the tumor volume grew to approximately 100 mm3. Images were observed and captured at the following time points: 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, and 24 h using a real-time fluorescence imaging system. The results were shown in Fig. 8a. It is found that the drug was widely distributed in the mice's body, including the tumor site, 2 h after administration. At 4 h, the accumulation at the tumor site was more pronounced, with fluorescence intensity significantly higher than in other areas. After 6 h of administration, the amount of the drug in the tumor reached its peak, exceeding the amount in other parts of the body. It significantly decreased after 8 h and further reduced after 12 h. After 24 h, the drug was noticeably diminished in the tumor site, but some accumulation remains. Fig. 8b showed the photothermal imaging of tumor temperature in mice after administration. The light source wavelength was 808 nm, with a power of 50 W cm−2, and the illumination time was10 min. It was observed that the temperature at the tumor site and other parts of the body began to rise 2 h after administration, reached a peak temperature at 6 h, at which point the temperature of the tumor was significantly higher than that of other body parts. Fig. 8c and d was quantitative histograms of in vivo fluorescence intensity and temperature, respectively. The fluorescence imaging and photothermal imaging were consistently, indicating that the drug accumulation at the tumor site peaked 6 h after tail vein administration, suggesting an optimal therapeutic window at 6 h post-administration. Based on the imaging-indicated therapeutic window, the mice were euthanized 6 h after administration to analyze the distribution of the drug in the tumors, heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys. Fig. 8e and f shown that the amount at the tumor site was highest 6 h after administration, followed by the liver and kidneys. Finally, based on the results of in vivo imaging and drug distribution, the optimal therapeutic window was determined to be 6 h post-administration. The following solid tumor phototherapy was scheduled for 6 h after tail vein administration.

Using 7-week-old female Balb/c mice as the treatment model, the treatment plan is illustrated in Fig. 9a. The mice were randomly divided into 5 groups: Saline group, HA@HMCaO2 group, HA@CuLCDs group, HACOCLC dark group, and HACOCLC+Laser group. The CT26 cells were inoculated on the back of mice, and treatment commenced when the tumor volume reached approximately 100 mm3. According to Fig. 9a, the dosage of HACOCLC was 6 mg kg−1. According to the load rate, the doses of HA@HMCaO2 and HA@CuLCDs were determined to be 2.14 mg kg−1 and 0.77 mg kg−1, respectively. The drug was administered via the tail artery on day 0, day 5, and day 10. Laser treatment was applied for 10 min after 6 h administration, concluding on day 14. The solid tumors were excised and photographed with a digital camera (Fig. 9b). It was observed that the solid tumors decrease sequentially according to the aforementioned groupings. The volume change curves of the solid tumors during treatment was shown in Fig. 9c. Upon analyzing the solid tumors and their volumes, it was found that tumors growth in the saline group were uncontrolled. Further analysis revealed that the solid tumors in the HA@HMCaO2 group were smaller than those in the control group, with a growth inhibition rate of 25.3 %, indicating that HA@HMCaO2 induced calcium overload and a cascaded reaction that led to apoptosis of CT26 cells, thereby inhibiting tumor growth. The tumor growth inhibition rate in the HA@CuLCDs group was 52.3 %, suggesting that the standalone use of CDT at the dosage in this study induced apoptosis of CT26 cells, with a significantly higher tumor growth inhibition effect than calcium overload. The inhibition rate in the HACOCLC dark group was 77 %. In this treatment group, there was apoptosis due to calcium overload, CDT-induced apoptosis, GSH depletion enhancing CDT, and CaO2 compensating for H2O2 to enhance CDT, all of which collectively inhibit tumor growth. The HACOCLC+laser group exhibited the highest inhibition rate at 98.43 %, which was 21.43 % higher than that of the HACOCLC dark group, indicating that the heating effect (PTT) of photothermal conversion significantly enhance the anti-tumor effect. In summary, HACOCLC demonstrated excellent multi-enhancement of CDT in vivo anti-tumor therapy.

The body weight of the mice shows a slight increase during the treatment period, indicating good growth with no significant adverse drug reactions (Fig. 9d). Fig. 9e shown the histological H&E staining results of the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys, and tumors. The tissues of each organ were dense, regular, intact, and clearly textured, with no damage observed, indicating that HACOCLC had good biocompatibility. In contrast, the degree of tumor tissue damage gradually increased according to the treatment sequence of HA@HMCaO2, HA@CuLCDs, HACOCLC dark group, and HACOCLC+Laser, with the last two groups shown the most severe damage. The treatment with HACOCLC had been demonstrated to be temporally and spatially selective. The aforementioned in vivo treatment results confirm that HACOCLC, based on multi-pathway enhanced CDT therapy, is successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo self-supply of H2O2/O2 and downregulation of HIF-1α/PD-L1
ce:section.ce:para: To further validate the ability of HACOCLC to self-donate H2O2/O2 and cascade downregulate HIF-1α and PD-L1 in vivo, we tested H2O2, HIF-1α, and PD-L1 within tumors. Firstly, the effect of CaO2 on the concentration of H2O2 in tumor tissues is studied. To avoid the influence of the Cu-Fenton reaction, copper-free HA@HMCaO2@LCDs and copper-containing HACOCLC are used at the same dosage, and the photothermal effect of the 808 nm laser was investigated. The results were shown in Fig. 10a. The study found that the H2O2 level in the no-light group of HA@HMCaO2@LCDs was 183 % higher than that of the control group, while the light group was 217 % higher than the control group. It was confirmed that the H2O2 release from the hydrolysis of CaO2 significantly increase the H2O2 concentration, and photothermal assistance raise the H2O2 level by 33.7 %. The H2O2 levels in the no-light group of HACOCLC was 133 % higher than those of the control group, and the light group was 167 % higher. In the HACOCLC group, there were both a process of HMCaO2 increasing H2O2 and a process of H2O2 consumption by the Cu-Fenton reaction, with these two processes competing against each other, where the former's competitive outcome was stronger than the latter's. This was because the concentration of CaO2 was very high in the early stages of the reaction, leading to an increase in the H2O2 storm. However, as CaO2 continue to be consumed, the H2O2 storm weaken, and the H2O2 consumption process of the Cu-Fenton reaction became dominant until H2O2 was completely consumed. The H2O2 levels in HACOCLC were slightly elevated in the light group. In summary, both HA@CaO2@LCDs and HACOCLC can significantly increase the concentration of H2O2 in tumor tissues.

In Fig. 10b,d, the immunofluorescence imaging of HIF-1α and PD-L1 in tumor tissues shown that the fluorescence intensity of HIF-1α and PD-L1 significantly decreases with the increase in concentrations of HA@CuLCDs, HA@HMCaO2@LCDs and HACOCLC (Fig. 10c and e), particularly the downregulation in the HACOCLC group were greater than that in the HA@CuLCDs and HA@HMCaO2@LCDs group, confirming a significant increase in O2 concentration in the tissues.

In summary, we successfully synthesized the multifunctional nano-reactor HACOCLC based on a multi-faceted strategy to enhance CDT. HACOCLC was constructed through co-assembly of the Schiff base(HA-CA), HMCaO2, and CuLCDs. HACOCLC targets accumulation at tumor sites and released HMCaO2 and CuLCDs pH stimulation. This solves the limitation of CDT due to insufficient H2O2. By in situ self-supplying H2O2/O2, calcium overload, photothermal effects, GSH consumption, and downregulation of HIF-1α/PD-L1, the anti-tumor effect was significantly improved. In vitro and in vivo experimental results indicated that HACOCLC exhibited good biocompatibility and significant tumor growth inhibition characteristics. This study presented an innovative hyaluronic acid Schiff base/CaO2/carbon dot nanocomposite, providing a promising avenue for ROS-related cancer therapy.
